cases.title: The Vigorous Newborn
cases.presentation: You arrive to assist with an unplanned home delivery. The term newborn is immediately crying vigorously, with good tone and pink color.
cases.question: What is the appropriate management for this newborn?
options: A: Move the newborn to a warmer for the initial steps of stabilization.
B: The newborn can stay with the mother for routine care — dry, warm, and skin-to-skin.
C: Begin positive-pressure ventilation as a precaution.
D: Perform routine deep suctioning before any other action.
cases.explanation: All three initial questions (term gestation, good tone, breathing/crying) are answered yes, so the newborn can remain with the mother for routine care rather than being moved to a warmer for stabilization steps.
cases.clinicalPearl: Term, good tone, vigorous cry — three yeses means routine care with mom, not intervention.

cases.title: The Limp, Quiet Newborn
cases.presentation: A newborn delivered five minutes ago is limp, not crying, and has poor tone. The newborn is wet and uncovered on the bed.
cases.question: What is the correct initial management sequence?
options: A: Leave the newborn with the mother and reassess in 10 minutes.
B: Move to a warmer and begin the initial steps: provide warmth, position the airway, clear secretions if needed, dry, and stimulate.
D: Delay any action until a full assessment is complete.
cases.explanation: Poor tone and absence of crying are 'no' answers to the initial questions, indicating the newborn should be moved to a warmer to begin the initial steps of stabilization — warmth, airway positioning, secretion clearing if needed, drying, and stimulation — with warmth being a particular priority since the newborn is currently wet and uncovered.
cases.clinicalPearl: Wet, limp, and quiet — get this newborn to the warmer and start the initial steps without delay; heat loss is happening right now.

cases.title: Persistent Bradycardia Despite Ventilation
cases.presentation: After the initial steps, a newborn remains apneic with a heart rate of 90 bpm. Positive-pressure ventilation is started. After 30 seconds of effective PPV, the heart rate is reassessed at 50 bpm.
cases.question: What is the correct next step?
options: A: Continue PPV alone without adding any other intervention.
B: Begin chest compressions coordinated with continued PPV, since the heart rate remains below 60 bpm despite effective ventilation.
cases.explanation: Because the heart rate remains below 60 bpm despite 30 seconds of effective PPV, chest compressions are added, coordinated with continued ventilation, per the newborn resuscitation sequence.
cases.clinicalPearl: Ventilation first, always — but once you've given it 30 good seconds and the heart rate is still under 60, add compressions.
